enrichment.short_description: Backlog is a project management and bug-tracking platform from Nulab that combines task management, Kanban boards, Gantt charts, and built-in Git and Subversion repositories for software and cross-functional teams.
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 200 employees company-wide (Nulab Inc.; estimate, varies by source)
enrichment.headquarters: Fukuoka, Japan (Nulab Inc.), with additional offices in Tokyo, Kyoto, New York, and Amsterdam
enrichment.seo_title: Backlog by Nulab Review 2026: Features, Pricing, Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Backlog combines task tracking, Kanban boards, Gantt charts, and Git/SVN hosting. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and who it fits best.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Backlog is a web-based project management and issue-tracking tool built by Nulab Inc., a Japanese software company founded in 2004 and headquartered in Fukuoka, Japan. Backlog itself was first released in Japan in 2005 and launched in English for a global audience in 2011. It targets software teams and cross-functional teams (marketing, design, operations) that want task management, Kanban-style boards, Gantt charts, burndown charts, wikis, and file storage bundled with built-in Git and Subversion repository hosting in one product, positioning it as an alternative to pairing Jira with a separate code host. According to Nulab press materials, Backlog has surpassed 10,000 paying customers and over 1.7 million registered users worldwide. Pricing is freemium: a Free plan supports up to 10 users, 1 project, and 100MB of storage. Paid tiers are Starter ($35/month, 30 users, 5 projects, 1GB storage, subtasking), Standard ($100/month, unlimited users, 100 projects, 30GB storage, Gantt and burndown charts, issue templates), Premium ($175/month, unlimited projects, 100GB storage, unlimited-range Gantt view, custom fields, and a bundled AI Assistant with 2,000 monthly credits), and a custom-priced Enterprise tier adding SAML single sign-on, SCIM user provisioning, audit logs, and a dedicated customer success manager. Notably, Backlog does not charge per-user fees on its higher tiers, which differentiates its pricing model from many project management competitors. Core features include Kanban and list-view boards, Gantt charts, burndown charts, custom issue statuses and workflows, subtasking, wikis, file attachments, integrated Git/SVN repositories with pull-request code review, watch and notification systems, and native mobile apps for iOS and Android. Nulab also makes complementary products, including Cacoo for diagramming and Typetalk for team chat, which integrate with Backlog. Backlog competes most directly with Jira, Trello, Asana, and ClickUp, and is frequently chosen by teams wanting a flatter learning curve than Jira while still supporting developer workflows like in-app code review. Nulab maintains offices in Fukuoka (headquarters), Tokyo, Kyoto, New York, Amsterdam, and Singapore. Exact current total employee counts vary by source (LinkedIn lists 51-200, PitchBook reports approximately 207), so this figure should be treated as an approximation rather than an exact number.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Backlog
paragraphs: Backlog is a project management and bug-tracking application made by Nulab Inc., a Japanese software company founded in 2004 in Fukuoka, Japan. The product was first launched domestically in Japan in 2005 and released in English for international users in 2011, and it has since grown into one of Nulab's flagship products alongside the diagramming tool Cacoo and the team chat app Typetalk.

The core idea behind Backlog is to combine task and project management with source code hosting in a single application, so that developers, designers, marketers, and project managers can track work items, discuss issues, and manage Git or Subversion repositories without switching between separate tools like Jira and GitHub.

Backlog is used by both technical and non-technical teams. Software teams use it for sprint planning, bug tracking, and code review, while marketing, HR, and operations teams use its boards and Gantt charts for general project coordination, which is part of why Nulab markets it as suitable for 'virtual teams' beyond pure software development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Backlog centers on issues, which can represent tasks, bugs, or requests and can be organized on Kanban-style boards, in list views, or on Gantt charts that show dependencies and timelines. Burndown charts and milestone tracking help teams monitor sprint or release progress at a glance.

A distinguishing feature is native Git and Subversion repository hosting built directly into each project, complete with pull requests, inline code review comments, and commit-to-issue linking, so code changes and the tasks that drove them stay connected without a third-party integration.

Beyond issue tracking, Backlog includes project wikis for documentation, file storage and attachments, custom issue types and workflows, subtasking, watch and notification settings, and mobile apps for iOS and Android, plus (on paid tiers) an AI Assistant intended to help draft issue text and summarize activity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Backlog offers a genuinely usable Free plan (1 project, 10 users, 100MB storage) alongside four paid tiers: Starter at $35/month (30 users, 5 projects, 1GB storage), Standard at $100/month (unlimited users, 100 projects, 30GB storage, Gantt and burndown charts), Premium at $175/month (unlimited projects, 100GB storage, custom fields, bundled AI Assistant credits), and a custom-quoted Enterprise plan with SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, and audit logging.
Unlike many competitors, Backlog's paid plans (Standard and above) do not cap or charge per user, which can make it noticeably cheaper than seat-based tools like Jira for larger teams.

Nulab offers a 30-day free trial of paid plans and roughly 17 percent savings for annual billing versus monthly billing. Additional AI credit packages can be purchased on top of the Premium and Enterprise plans.

features.title: Kanban and list-view boards
features.description: Visualize and move issues through custom workflow statuses on drag-and-drop boards or in sortable list views.

features.title: Gantt charts and burndown charts
features.description: Plan timelines, dependencies, and milestones, and track sprint or release progress with built-in burndown reporting.

features.title: Integrated Git and Subversion hosting
features.description: Host repositories directly inside each Backlog project with pull requests and inline code review tied to issues.

features.title: Custom issue types and workflows
features.description: Define custom issue types, statuses, priorities, and fields to match a team's own process rather than a fixed template.

features.title: Project wikis
features.description: Maintain shared documentation, specs, and notes alongside the tasks and code they relate to.

features.title: Subtasking and dependencies
features.description: Break larger issues into subtasks and link related or blocking issues for clearer sequencing of work.

features.title: AI Assistant (Premium and Enterprise)
features.description: A bundled AI feature with a monthly credit allowance intended to help draft or summarize issue content.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps for tracking issues and receiving notifications away from the desktop.

pros: No per-user pricing on Standard tier and above, which keeps costs predictable as teams grow
Combines task management with native Git/SVN hosting, reducing the need for a separate code host
Simpler and flatter learning curve than heavyweight tools like Jira
Usable free plan for very small teams, not just a time-limited trial
Strong Gantt chart and milestone support for teams that need visual timeline planning
cons: Less extensible than Jira for teams that need deep customization or a large third-party marketplace
Free plan is limited to a single project and 10 users, which small growing teams may outgrow quickly
Storage limits on lower tiers can be restrictive for teams attaching large files or media
Smaller ecosystem of integrations compared to more dominant project management platforms
Repository features, while convenient, are less full-featured than dedicated platforms like GitHub or GitLab

faqs.question: Is Backlog free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Backlog offers a permanently free plan for up to 10 users, 1 project, and 100MB of storage. Paid plans start at $35/month for teams that need more projects, users, or storage.

faqs.question: Does Backlog include Git hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every Backlog project can include a built-in Git or Subversion repository with pull requests and code review, so teams do not need a separate code hosting service for basic use.

faqs.question: Who makes Backlog?
faqs.answer: Backlog is made by Nulab Inc., a Japanese software company founded in 2004 and headquartered in Fukuoka, Japan, which also makes the diagramming tool Cacoo and the chat app Typetalk.

faqs.question: Does Backlog charge per user?
faqs.answer: No, on the Standard, Premium, and Enterprise tiers. Only the Starter plan has a hard user cap (30 users); higher tiers support unlimited users at a flat monthly price.

faqs.question: Is Backlog good for non-technical teams?
faqs.answer: Yes. While it includes developer-oriented features like code repositories, Backlog's boards, Gantt charts, and wikis are commonly used by marketing, design, and operations teams as a general project management tool.

faqs.question: What is included in the Backlog AI Assistant?
faqs.answer: The AI Assistant is bundled with the Premium and Enterprise plans and includes a monthly credit allowance (2,000 credits by default); additional credit packages can be purchased separately.

faqs.question: How is Backlog different from Jira?
faqs.answer: Backlog aims for a simpler setup and flatter learning curve than Jira, bundles code hosting natively, and does not charge per user on its higher tiers, though it offers less deep customization and a smaller integration ecosystem than Jira.

faqs.question: Is there an annual discount for Backlog?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nulab offers roughly a 17 percent discount for annual billing compared to paying monthly.

use_cases.title: Software development bug tracking
use_cases.description: Development teams use Backlog to log bugs, link them to code commits and pull requests, and track resolution through custom workflows.

use_cases.title: Cross-functional project coordination
use_cases.description: Marketing, design, and operations teams use Backlog's boards and Gantt charts to plan campaigns and projects without needing developer-focused tools.

use_cases.title: Small agency and consultancy project management
use_cases.description: Agencies use Backlog to manage multiple client projects with separate boards, wikis, and permissions, while keeping code and tasks connected for technical deliverables.

enrichment.short_description: Barracuda Networks is a cybersecurity vendor offering email security, application and cloud security, network security (including SecureEdge SASE and CloudGen Firewall), and data backup products, sold mainly through managed service providers.
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 1,500 to 2,000 employees (estimate; exact current figure not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Barracuda Networks Review 2026: Products, Pricing, Owner
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Barracuda Networks security suite covers email, network, and backup protection. See 2026 ownership (KKR), pricing approach, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Barracuda Networks is a cybersecurity company founded in 2003 by Dean Drako, Michael Perone, and Zach Levow, headquartered in Campbell, California. The company built its early reputation on spam and email security appliances before expanding into a broad security portfolio spanning email protection, application and API security (including web application firewall-as-a-service), network security (CloudGen Firewall and the SecureEdge SASE platform), and data backup and disaster recovery, sold predominantly through a channel of managed service providers (MSPs) and resellers rather than direct enterprise sales alone. Barracuda went public on the New York Stock Exchange in 2013 under the ticker CUDA. In 2018, private equity firm Thoma Bravo acquired Barracuda and took it private in a deal reported at roughly 1.6 billion dollars. Thoma Bravo owned Barracuda for about four years, during which the company expanded its product suite and grew revenue to over 500 million dollars annually. In April 2022, KKR announced a definitive agreement to acquire Barracuda from Thoma Bravo; the transaction closed in August 2022, with financial terms not publicly disclosed. As of the most recent verifiable reporting, Barracuda Networks is owned by KKR, which has stated it implemented a broad-based employee ownership program making Barracuda employees co-owners of the business. Barracuda does not publish self-service list pricing for most of its products; instead, pricing is quote-based and varies by product line, deployment size, and whether it is purchased directly or through an MSP or channel partner, which is standard practice in enterprise and mid-market cybersecurity. Barracuda's customer base skews toward small and mid-sized businesses and the MSPs that serve them, differentiating it from security vendors that focus primarily on large enterprise accounts. Employee counts vary across third-party sources (roughly 1,500 to 2,000 or more depending on source and date), so this figure should be treated as an approximation. This entry reflects publicly reported ownership and business facts as of mid-2026; readers should verify current ownership independently for any transaction-critical purpose, since further ownership changes are always possible in private equity-held companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Barracuda Networks
paragraphs: Barracuda Networks is a cybersecurity company founded in 2003 and headquartered in Campbell, California. It began with spam-filtering and email security appliances and has since grown into a broader security portfolio covering email, applications, networks, and data backup.

The company primarily serves small and mid-sized businesses, often reaching them through a network of managed service providers (MSPs) and resellers rather than relying solely on direct enterprise sales, which distinguishes it from security vendors focused almost exclusively on large enterprise accounts.

Barracuda has changed ownership more than once. It was a publicly traded company (NYSE: CUDA) from 2013 until Thoma Bravo took it private in 2018; Thoma Bravo then sold Barracuda to KKR in a deal announced in April 2022 and completed in August 2022. As of the most recent public reporting, KKR owns Barracuda Networks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Barracuda's product lines are typically grouped into four areas: Email Protection (anti-phishing, spam filtering, impersonation and account takeover protection), Application Protection (web application and API security delivered as a service), Network Protection (CloudGen Firewall and the SecureEdge SASE platform for secure remote access), and Data Protection (backup and disaster recovery appliances and cloud services).

Many of Barracuda's offerings are designed to be managed centrally, which appeals to MSPs handling security for many small business clients at once, and the company has also expanded into extended detection and response (XDR) style managed security services.

Because Barracuda sells a portfolio of distinct security products rather than a single unified suite, most customers select and license individual product lines based on their needs rather than a single blanket 'Barracuda' subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Barracuda does not publish self-service list pricing for most products. Pricing is quote-based, driven by factors such as the specific product line, number of users or mailboxes protected, appliance or virtual instance sizing, and contract length.

A significant share of Barracuda's business flows through MSPs and channel partners, who often bundle Barracuda products into their own managed security offerings, which further means end pricing can vary by reseller.
Prospective buyers typically need to request a quote or demo directly from Barracuda or an authorized partner to get current pricing for a specific product and deployment size.

features.title: Email Protection
features.description: Anti-phishing, spam filtering, and impersonation/account-takeover protection for cloud and on-premises email systems.

features.title: Application and API Protection
features.description: Web application firewall-as-a-service and API security to protect internet-facing applications from attacks.

features.title: CloudGen Firewall
features.description: Next-generation firewall appliances and virtual instances for on-premises and cloud network security.

features.title: SecureEdge SASE platform
features.description: Secure access service edge platform combining SD-WAN and zero trust network access for distributed workforces.

features.title: Data backup and disaster recovery
features.description: Backup appliances and cloud-based backup services for servers, endpoints, and Microsoft 365 data.

features.title: Managed XDR services
features.description: Extended detection and response offerings aimed at giving smaller organizations access to 24/7 threat monitoring.

features.title: MSP-oriented management tools
features.description: Centralized management consoles designed for managed service providers overseeing many client environments.

pros: Broad security portfolio covering email, app, network, and backup under one vendor
Long operating history (since 2003) with an established channel of MSP partners
Products aimed specifically at small and mid-sized business needs, not just large enterprises
Backing from major private equity investors (KKR) suggests continued investment capacity
Centralized management tools that suit MSPs managing many clients
cons: No public list pricing, making upfront budgeting harder without contacting sales
Product portfolio is broad but not always as deeply integrated as a purpose-built single-vendor suite
Repeated ownership changes (public to Thoma Bravo to KKR) can create uncertainty about long-term product roadmaps
Primarily channel/MSP-oriented sales model may not suit buyers who prefer direct self-service purchasing
As with any large security vendor, individual product lines vary in maturity and depth compared to point-solution specialists

faqs.question: Who owns Barracuda Networks in 2026?
faqs.answer: Based on public reporting, Barracuda Networks is owned by the investment firm KKR, which acquired the company from Thoma Bravo in a deal announced in April 2022 and completed in August 2022.

faqs.question: Was Barracuda ever publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes. Barracuda Networks was listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker CUDA following its 2013 IPO, until Thoma Bravo took it private in 2018.

faqs.question: When was Barracuda Networks founded?
faqs.answer: Barracuda Networks was founded in 2003 by Dean Drako, Michael Perone, and Zach Levow.

faqs.question: Where is Barracuda Networks headquartered?
faqs.answer: Barracuda Networks is headquartered in Campbell, California, USA.

faqs.question: What products does Barracuda Networks sell?
faqs.answer: Barracuda's main product areas are email security, application and API security, network security (CloudGen Firewall and SecureEdge SASE), and data backup/disaster recovery, along with managed XDR security services.

faqs.question: How much does Barracuda cost?
faqs.answer: Barracuda does not publish standard list pricing for most products; pricing is quote-based and depends on the specific product, deployment size, and whether it is purchased directly or through a partner/MSP.

faqs.question: Does Barracuda sell to individuals or only businesses?
faqs.answer: Barracuda's products are built for organizations, primarily small and mid-sized businesses, and are frequently delivered through managed service providers rather than sold to individual consumers.

faqs.question: Is Barracuda a good fit for managed service providers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Barracuda has historically positioned much of its portfolio, and its centralized management tooling, around MSPs that resell and manage security for multiple small business clients.

use_cases.title: Business email security
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy Barracuda Email Protection to filter phishing, spam, and business email compromise attempts across cloud email platforms like Microsoft 365.
use_cases.audience: IT and security teams at small and mid-sized businesses

use_cases.title: MSP-delivered managed security
use_cases.description: Managed service providers use Barracuda's centralized tools to deliver email, network, and backup security as a bundled service to many small business clients.

use_cases.title: Secure remote access and network protection
use_cases.description: Distributed organizations use CloudGen Firewall and SecureEdge to secure branch and remote-worker network access under a SASE model.
use_cases.audience: IT teams with distributed or remote workforces

enrichment.short_description: BasKet Note Pads is a free, open-source note-organizing application, originally built for KDE on Linux, that lets users collect text, images, links, and files into hierarchical, scrapbook-style baskets.
enrichment.seo_title: BasKet Note Pads: Free Open-Source Notes App (2026 Guide)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BasKet Note Pads is a free, open-source KDE note organizer for text, images, links and files. See features, history, and current 2026 status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BasKet Note Pads, commonly written as Basket, is a free and open-source note-organizing application originally created for the KDE desktop environment on Linux. Copyright notices in the project date to 2003-2006 under original author Sebastien Laout, indicating the project began around 2003, and it was designed conceptually as a KDE equivalent to the classic Mac scrapbook-style note tool DropDrawers. The application lets users collect and organize free-form information, including plain text, rich text, images, links, email addresses, files, application launchers, and even colors picked from the screen, into 'baskets' that can be nested hierarchically by topic or project, similar to a digital scrapbook or bulletin board rather than a strictly linear notebook. Notes within a basket can be laid out automatically in columns or positioned freely by the user. The application also supports tagging notes with labels such as Important, Information, Idea, Work, or Personal, and supports multi-state tags that function as checkboxes for to-do lists, progress bars, or priority indicators. BasKet can import data from several other note applications, including KNotes, KJots, KnowIt, TuxCards, Sticky Notes, and Tomboy, which made it a common migration target for Linux users consolidating scattered notes. The project is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2) and its source code is publicly available, including a maintained mirror on GitHub under the KDE organization and a community fork at basket-notepads.github.io. Development slowed for a period as KDE moved from KDE 4 to Plasma/KDE Frameworks, but the project has seen renewed activity, including a rewrite targeting Qt6 and KDE Frameworks 6, and it remains listed on the official KDE Store and KDE Applications catalog as of the most recent available information. As a community-maintained open-source project rather than a commercial product, BasKet has no company, headquarters, or formal pricing; it is free to download and use, and its ongoing development depends on volunteer contributors rather than a dedicated business. Exact current maintenance activity levels and release cadence should be verified directly against the project's GitHub repository for anyone relying on up-to-the-day status.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BasKet Note Pads
paragraphs: BasKet Note Pads is a free, open-source note-taking and organizing application that originated on the KDE desktop for Linux. Its copyright notices trace back to 2003 under original author Sebastien Laout, and it was conceived as a KDE-native equivalent to the Mac scrapbook tool DropDrawers.

Rather than a simple linear notebook, BasKet organizes information into 'baskets' that can hold mixed content, plain text, images, links, files, and more, and can be nested hierarchically to mirror how a user actually organizes topics or projects.

The project is entirely community-maintained and released under the GPLv2 open-source license, with no company or commercial entity behind it. It is distributed through Linux package managers, the KDE Store, and source repositories on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: BasKet supports pasting or dragging in a wide range of content types into a basket, including rich text, images, links, email addresses, files, application launchers, colors, and screen captures, making it more of a flexible information collector than a plain-text note app.

Notes can be tagged with labels like Important, Information, Idea, Work, or Personal, and multi-state tags allow notes to double as checkboxes, progress indicators, or priority markers, giving BasKet lightweight to-do list functionality alongside free-form notes.

The application includes import tools for migrating data from other note applications, including KNotes, KJots, KnowIt, TuxCards, Sticky Notes, and Tomboy, which historically made it a common consolidation point for Linux desktop users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BasKet Note Pads is entirely free to download and use, with no paid tier, subscription, or license fee, consistent with its status as a volunteer-maintained open-source KDE project.
Because it is open source under the GPLv2 license, the source code is publicly available for anyone to inspect, modify, or redistribute, and there is no vendor to purchase support or upgrades from.

Users install it through Linux distribution package managers, the KDE Store, or by building it from source, and any costs involved are limited to the general costs of running Linux hardware rather than the software itself.

features.title: Hierarchical baskets
features.description: Organize notes into nested baskets by topic or project rather than a flat list of notes.

features.title: Mixed content notes
features.description: Store text, images, links, files, email addresses, and application launchers within the same basket.

features.title: Tagging and multi-state tags
features.description: Attach labels like Important or Idea, or use multi-state tags for checkboxes, progress bars, and priority markers.

features.title: Flexible or automatic layout
features.description: Let BasKet auto-arrange notes in columns or position them freely on the canvas.

features.title: Import from other note apps
features.description: Migrate existing notes from KNotes, KJots, KnowIt, TuxCards, Sticky Notes, or Tomboy.

features.title: Screen capture and color picking
features.description: Grab a screen region or pick a color directly from the screen into a note.

features.title: Open-source GPLv2 codebase
features.description: Fully open source, allowing community inspection, modification, and redistribution.

pros: Completely free with no paid tier or licensing cost
Flexible scrapbook-style organization suited to messy, mixed-format information
Supports migrating notes in from several other legacy note applications
Open-source codebase allows community fixes and forks to keep it alive
Lightweight to-do functionality via multi-state tags without needing a separate task app
cons: Primarily built for the Linux/KDE desktop, with limited native support on Windows and macOS
Development pace has been inconsistent over the years compared to commercial note apps
Interface and feature set feel dated compared to modern note apps like Notion or Obsidian
No official cloud sync or mobile app maintained by the core project
Documentation and community support are smaller than for mainstream commercial alternatives

faqs.question: Is BasKet Note Pads free?
faqs.answer: Yes. BasKet Note Pads is free, open-source software released under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What platform does BasKet Note Pads run on?
faqs.answer: BasKet Note Pads was built for the KDE desktop environment on Linux; it is distributed through Linux package managers and the KDE Store.

faqs.question: Who created BasKet Note Pads?
faqs.answer: BasKet was originally created by Sebastien Laout, with copyright notices dating the project's origin to around 2003, and has since had contributions from other developers.

faqs.question: Can BasKet import notes from other apps?
faqs.answer: Yes. BasKet can import data from several other note applications, including KNotes, KJots, KnowIt, TuxCards, Sticky Notes, and Tomboy.

faqs.question: Is BasKet Note Pads still maintained?
faqs.answer: The project has had periods of slower activity, but it remains listed on the KDE Store and KDE Applications catalog and has seen renewed development including a port targeting Qt6 and KDE Frameworks 6.

faqs.question: What kind of content can I store in a basket?
faqs.answer: A basket can hold plain and rich text, images, links, email addresses, files, application launchers, and colors picked from the screen.

faqs.question: Does BasKet support to-do lists?
faqs.answer: It supports lightweight to-do functionality through multi-state tags that can act as checkboxes, progress bars, or priority indicators on notes.

faqs.question: Is there a company behind BasKet Note Pads?
faqs.answer: No. BasKet is a volunteer, community-maintained open-source project with no company, headquarters, or commercial backing.

use_cases.title: Personal knowledge scrapbooking
use_cases.description: Individuals use BasKet to collect mixed-format notes, links, and images into organized baskets for personal reference.

use_cases.title: Legacy note migration on Linux
use_cases.description: Users switching from older note tools like Tomboy or KNotes import their existing notes into BasKet to consolidate them in one app.
use_cases.audience: Linux users migrating from other note apps

use_cases.title: Lightweight project note organization
use_cases.description: Small projects use nested baskets to keep project-related notes, links, and files grouped by topic without a full project management tool.
use_cases.audience: Hobbyists and small open-source project maintainers

enrichment.short_description: Bastet is a free, open-source, ncurses-based falling-block puzzle game for Linux and Unix terminals that deliberately gives players the worst possible piece instead of a random one, making it intentionally harder than standard Tetris-style games.
enrichment.seo_title: Bastet: The Evil Terminal Tetris Clone (2026 Guide)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bastet is a free open-source terminal Tetris clone that deliberately gives you the worst piece. See how it works, features, and where to get it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bastet, short for 'bastard tetris', is a free and open-source, ncurses-based falling-block puzzle game for Linux and Unix terminals, created by Federico Poloni. It is functionally similar to Tetris, but instead of choosing the next piece at random, Bastet deliberately uses a scoring algorithm to select the worst possible piece for the player's current board state, intentionally making the game harder and more frustrating than standard Tetris-style clones. Bastet's own tagline describes it as an attractive alternative to Microsoft Word for people who enjoy swearing at their computer. The game offers two modes: a 'normal' mode that still shows a preview of the next piece (with the adversarial algorithm working around that preview), and a 'harder' mode that hides the next-piece preview entirely, in which the piece-selection algorithm is further tuned to exploit the lack of a preview. According to the project's own release history, the piece-selection algorithm evolved over multiple versions, from an initial approach in early releases (version 0.37 was the first public release) to a more sophisticated rewritten algorithm in version 0.43 (2009) that also included a correction mechanism to avoid selecting the same piece too many times in a row, which would otherwise make the adversarial pattern too obvious or 'implausible' to players. The most recent versions found in the project's public source history are in the 0.43.x range, with a tagged release (0.43.2) from June 2017; a Slashdot community post about the game titled 'Bastard Tetris Hates You' dates to April 2005, indicating the game already existed and had a public following by that time, though an exact founding or first-release year could not be independently confirmed and should not be treated as certain. Bastet is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), and its source code (written primarily in C++) is publicly hosted on GitHub under the fph/bastet repository, which as of research had over 300 stars and dozens of forks, reflecting a modest but persistent following among terminal and retro-gaming enthusiasts. The game is packaged in most major Linux distributions' repositories and is also portable to other Unix-like systems and, per some packaging notes, Windows and macOS via ncurses-compatible environments. As a solo open-source hobby project rather than a commercial product, Bastet has no company, headquarters, employee count, or pricing plans; it is entirely free.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bastet
paragraphs: Bastet, short for 'bastard tetris', is a free, open-source, terminal-based falling-block puzzle game created by Federico Poloni. It plays like a classic Tetris clone but with one deliberate twist: the game does not select your next piece randomly.

Instead, Bastet uses a scoring algorithm designed to hand the player the least helpful piece available for their current board layout, intentionally working against the player rather than for them, which is the source of both its name and its reputation for being 'evil'.
The game runs in a terminal using the ncurses library, keeping it lightweight and dependency-free, and it is widely packaged across Linux distributions as a small, self-contained retro-style game.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The centerpiece of Bastet is its adversarial block-selection algorithm, which evaluates the current board and deliberately picks the piece least likely to help the player clear lines, rather than choosing randomly like standard Tetris implementations.

Bastet offers two difficulty modes: a normal mode that still shows a preview of the upcoming piece, and a harder mode that hides the preview entirely, with the selection algorithm further tuned to take advantage of the player not knowing what is coming next.

According to its own version history, the algorithm has been revised more than once, including a rewrite in version 0.43 that added logic to avoid repeating the same piece too many times in a row, keeping the adversarial pattern from becoming too obviously predictable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bastet is completely free and released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), with no paid version, in-app purchases, or subscription of any kind.
As open-source software, its full C++ source code is publicly available on GitHub, allowing anyone to inspect, modify, compile, or redistribute it under the terms of the GPL.
It is available through the package managers of most major Linux distributions, so most users can install it with a single package-manager command at no cost.

features.title: Adversarial piece selection
features.description: A scoring algorithm deliberately chooses the least helpful next piece instead of selecting randomly.

features.title: Two difficulty modes
features.description: A normal mode with a next-piece preview and a harder mode that hides the preview entirely.

features.title: Terminal-based ncurses interface
features.description: Runs entirely in a text terminal using the lightweight ncurses library, with no graphical dependencies.

features.title: Classic Tetris-style controls
features.description: Standard falling-block controls including movement, rotation, and hard-drop.

features.title: High score tracking
features.description: Keeps local high scores to encourage repeat play despite the game's intentional difficulty.

features.title: Cross-distribution packaging
features.description: Packaged in most major Linux distributions' repositories for simple installation.

features.title: Open-source C++ codebase
features.description: Publicly available source code under GPLv3 for community inspection and contribution.

pros: Completely free and open source with no ads, accounts, or purchases
Lightweight terminal game with minimal system requirements
Distinctive, genuinely challenging gameplay twist not found in standard Tetris clones
Widely packaged across Linux distributions for easy installation
Small but persistent community of contributors on GitHub
cons: Deliberately frustrating design will not appeal to players who want a fair, standard Tetris experience
Terminal-only interface lacks the visual polish of graphical Tetris clones
Development has been infrequent in recent years based on public release history
No official mobile app or online multiplayer
Exact founding/first-release date is not clearly documented, which limits precise historical claims

faqs.question: What does 'Bastet' stand for?
faqs.answer: Bastet stands for 'bastard tetris', reflecting the game's design goal of deliberately giving the player the worst possible piece.

faqs.question: Is Bastet free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bastet is completely free and open source, released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3).

faqs.question: How does Bastet choose pieces differently from Tetris?
faqs.answer: Instead of choosing the next piece randomly, Bastet uses a scoring algorithm that evaluates the board and deliberately selects the piece least likely to help the player clear lines.

faqs.question: Who created Bastet?
faqs.answer: Bastet was created by Federico Poloni, and its source code is maintained in the fph/bastet repository on GitHub.

faqs.question: What platforms does Bastet run on?
faqs.answer: Bastet is primarily built for Linux and Unix terminals using the ncurses library, and it is packaged in most major Linux distributions; ncurses-compatible builds exist for other Unix-like systems as well.

faqs.question: Does Bastet have difficulty options?
faqs.answer: Yes. It has a normal mode with a next-piece preview and a harder mode that hides the preview, with the piece-selection algorithm adjusted accordingly.

faqs.question: Is Bastet based on official Tetris?
faqs.answer: Bastet is an independent, unofficial falling-block puzzle game inspired by Tetris-style gameplay; it is not an officially licensed Tetris product.

faqs.question: Where can I download Bastet?
faqs.answer: Bastet can typically be installed via a Linux distribution's package manager, or built from source from its GitHub repository at fph/bastet.

use_cases.title: Terminal gaming on Linux
use_cases.description: Linux users install Bastet from their distribution's package manager for a lightweight, no-frills puzzle game in the terminal.

use_cases.title: Challenge-seeking Tetris fans
use_cases.description: Players who find standard Tetris too easy use Bastet's adversarial piece selection for a deliberately harder challenge.

use_cases.title: Retro/minimalist gaming on low-resource systems
use_cases.description: Users on minimal or resource-constrained systems (servers, old hardware, SSH sessions) run Bastet as a lightweight terminal diversion.

enrichment.short_description: BBEdit is a long-running macOS text and code editor from Bare Bones Software, known for powerful grep-based search and multi-file text manipulation, offered with a genuinely usable free mode alongside paid subscription and one-time license options.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Free Mode); paid plans from $4.99/month or $49.99/year
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held company (exact employee count not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: BBEdit Review 2026: Features, Pricing, Free Mode Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BBEdit is Bare Bones Software's macOS text and code editor. See 2026 pricing (Free Mode, subscription, one-time license), features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BBEdit is a macOS-native text and code editor made by Bare Bones Software, Inc., a privately held company founded by developer Rich Siegel. BBEdit was first released as freeware in 1992, originally intended as a demonstration of an experimental Pascal compiler for the Macintosh, before becoming a commercial product; Bare Bones Software itself was formally founded in May 1993 and incorporated in Massachusetts in June 1994. The company's own copyright notices span '1992-2026', reflecting over three decades of continuous development, and it is headquartered in North Chelmsford, Massachusetts. BBEdit has long marketed itself with the tagline 'It doesn't suck,' a registered trademark, emphasizing reliability and depth of text-manipulation features over flashiness. Its feature set includes powerful grep-based pattern search and replace across single files or entire multi-file projects, syntax coloring for a wide range of programming and markup languages, code folding, function/symbol navigation, FTP and SFTP file access, integrated Git and Subversion support, AppleScript and Unix shell scripting hooks, text and code completion, and dedicated HTML authoring tools. As of the most recent pricing found, BBEdit uses a mixed pricing model: a 'Free Mode' that unlocks automatically after a 30-day full trial period and can be used indefinitely at no cost with a reduced feature set (Bare Bones states there are no nag screens in this mode), plus paid access via a Mac App Store subscription (reported at $4.99 per month or $49.99 per year) or a traditional one-time commercial license purchased through Bare Bones' own online store, with upgrade pricing available for owners of older commercial licenses (BBEdit 15.5.5 or earlier was referenced as the upgrade-eligible baseline in current documentation). BBEdit requires macOS 14.0 or later in its current version and ships as a universal binary supporting both Intel and Apple silicon Macs. It is widely regarded within the Mac developer and technical writing community as one of the most durable and well-regarded text editors in the platform's history, competing today with editors like Sublime Text, Visual Studio Code, and Nova. Precise current company size (employee count) is not publicly disclosed, consistent with Bare Bones Software's status as a small, privately held software company.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BBEdit
paragraphs: BBEdit is a macOS-native text and code editor made by Bare Bones Software, Inc., founded by developer Rich Siegel. BBEdit was first released as freeware in 1992, and the company itself was formally established in 1993 and incorporated in Massachusetts in 1994, making BBEdit one of the longest continuously developed applications on the Mac platform.

The product is known within the Mac developer and publishing community for its depth of text-manipulation tools, particularly its grep-based search and replace, and for its long-standing trademarked tagline, 'It doesn't suck.'
Bare Bones Software is headquartered in North Chelmsford, Massachusetts, and remains a small, privately held company focused primarily on BBEdit alongside a small number of other Mac utilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: BBEdit's core strength is text manipulation at scale: grep-based pattern matching and replace across a single file or an entire multi-file project, combined with syntax coloring for a wide range of programming and markup languages and code folding for navigating large files.

It includes practical workflow tools such as FTP/SFTP file access for editing remote files directly, integrated Git and Subversion support for version control, and AppleScript and Unix shell scripting hooks for automating repetitive editing tasks.
For web and markup work, BBEdit provides dedicated HTML authoring tools, and more broadly it supports text and code completion and flexible project definitions for organizing related files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BBEdit offers a full-featured 30-day trial, after which it automatically drops into a 'Free Mode' that remains usable indefinitely at no cost, with a reduced but still practical feature set and no nag screens, according to Bare Bones' own description.

For full functionality, BBEdit is available as a subscription (reported pricing of $4.99 per month or $49.99 per year) through the Mac App Store, or as a traditional one-time commercial license purchased directly from Bare Bones Software's online store.

Owners of older commercial licenses (BBEdit 15.5.5 and earlier, per current documentation) can access upgrade pricing rather than paying full price again, reflecting Bare Bones' long-standing practice of rewarding existing customers.

features.title: Grep-based search and replace
features.description: Powerful pattern-based find and replace across single files or entire multi-file projects.

features.title: Syntax coloring for many languages
features.description: Built-in syntax highlighting for a wide range of programming and markup languages.

features.title: Code folding and navigation
features.description: Collapse code blocks and jump directly to functions or symbols in large files.

features.title: FTP and SFTP file access
features.description: Edit files directly on remote servers over FTP or SFTP without a separate client.

features.title: Git and Subversion integration
features.description: Built-in support for common version control operations without leaving the editor.

features.title: AppleScript and shell scripting hooks
features.description: Automate repetitive editing tasks using AppleScript or Unix shell scripts.

features.title: HTML authoring tools
features.description: Dedicated markup tools tailored to writing and validating HTML.

features.title: Free Mode after trial
features.description: A permanently usable free tier that activates automatically once the 30-day full trial ends.

pros: Genuinely usable free mode rather than a time-limited trial that stops working
Extremely deep, mature text-manipulation and grep search capabilities refined over three decades
Long track record of stability and continuous support on macOS
Flexible purchase options: subscription or traditional one-time license
Strong reputation and loyal following among long-time Mac developers and writers
cons: macOS-only, with no native Windows or Linux version
Free Mode has a reduced feature set compared to the paid version
Interface conventions can feel dated compared to newer editors like VS Code
Smaller extension/plugin ecosystem than some modern cross-platform editors
Requires a relatively recent macOS version (14.0 or later) for the current release

faqs.question: Is BBEdit free?
faqs.answer: BBEdit offers a full 30-day trial, after which it can be used indefinitely in a reduced-feature 'Free Mode' at no cost; full functionality requires a paid subscription or license.

faqs.question: How much does BBEdit cost?
faqs.answer: Reported pricing is $4.99 per month or $49.99 per year via Mac App Store subscription, with a traditional one-time commercial license also available directly from Bare Bones Software.

faqs.question: Who makes BBEdit?
faqs.answer: BBEdit is made by Bare Bones Software, Inc., founded by developer Rich Siegel and headquartered in North Chelmsford, Massachusetts.

faqs.question: When was BBEdit first released?
faqs.answer: BBEdit was first released as freeware in 1992; Bare Bones Software was formally founded in 1993 and incorporated in 1994.

faqs.question: Does BBEdit support Git?
faqs.answer: Yes, BBEdit includes integrated support for Git and Subversion version control.

faqs.question: Is BBEdit available on Windows?
faqs.answer: No, BBEdit is a macOS-native application only, with no official Windows or Linux version.

faqs.question: What does BBEdit's Free Mode include?
faqs.answer: Free Mode activates automatically after the 30-day trial ends and provides a reduced but still practical set of BBEdit's core features at no cost, with no nag screens according to Bare Bones.

faqs.question: Can I upgrade from an older BBEdit license?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bare Bones Software offers upgrade pricing for owners of older commercial licenses (BBEdit 15.5.5 or earlier, per current documentation) rather than requiring a full-price purchase.

use_cases.title: Web and markup development
use_cases.description: Developers use BBEdit's HTML authoring tools and syntax coloring to hand-edit web markup and related code.

use_cases.title: Large-scale text and code refactoring
use_cases.description: Technical teams use BBEdit's grep-based multi-file search and replace to make sweeping changes across many files at once.

use_cases.title: Remote server file editing
use_cases.description: System administrators and developers use BBEdit's built-in FTP/SFTP support to edit configuration and code files directly on remote servers.

features: Upgrade pricing available for prior owners

enrichment.short_description: Beampipe is a lightweight, cookie-less website analytics tool aimed at privacy-conscious site owners, offering a free tier for low-traffic sites and paid plans for higher page-view volumes.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 10,000 monthly pageviews and 5 domains); paid plans from $10/month
enrichment.seo_title: Beampipe Review 2026: Privacy Analytics Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Beampipe is a cookie-less, privacy-focused web analytics tool. See 2026 pricing, free tier limits, features, and how it compares to Google Analytics.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Beampipe is a privacy-focused, cookie-less website analytics tool positioned as a lightweight alternative to Google Analytics. Public discussion of the product on the Indie Hackers community dates to around August 2020, indicating it was built and launched by a small, independent team or founder around that time; a specific founder name could not be independently confirmed through available sources, so this should be treated as unverified rather than stated as fact. Beampipe markets itself around simplicity and privacy: it does not use cookies to track visitors and states that its tracking approach is designed to comply with GDPR, the UK's PECR, and the US's CCPA. Core functionality includes standard traffic reporting (visits, page views, referrers, and audience geography), custom event tracking for actions like sign-ups or purchases, goal tracking, and a Slack integration for notifications and daily/periodic reports delivered directly into a team's chat tool. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free plan supports up to 10,000 monthly page views across up to 5 domains with no credit card required, while the entry-level paid plan is priced at 10 dollars per month and covers up to 100,000 monthly page views across 20 domains, adding the Slack integration; based on available information, higher-volume paid tiers likely exist above this but could not be independently confirmed in detail during this research. As of research in 2026, the beampipe.io website, its documentation site, its blog, and an associated X (formerly Twitter) account for the product were all reachable and presenting current-looking content and pricing, which supports treating Beampipe as an actively operating service rather than a shut-down product, though this assessment is based on the site being live and not on independently confirmed, dated recent usage or company financial data. Because Beampipe appears to be a small, independently or team-run analytics product rather than a large company, verifiable public information about company size, headquarters, funding, or a formal legal entity is limited, and none of those specifics should be treated as confirmed facts; readers evaluating Beampipe for business-critical use should verify current operational status directly on beampipe.io before relying on it.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Beampipe
paragraphs: Beampipe is a privacy-focused, cookie-less website analytics tool built as a lightweight alternative to Google Analytics. Public discussion of the product on Indie Hackers dates to around August 2020, suggesting it launched around that time as a small, independently built product.

The tool's core pitch centers on privacy and simplicity: it avoids cookies for tracking visitors and describes its approach as designed to comply with major privacy regulations, including GDPR, the UK's PECR, and California's CCPA.

Beampipe fits into the broader wave of cookie-less, privacy-first analytics tools (alongside products like Plausible and Fathom) that emerged as an alternative to Google Analytics for site owners who want traffic insight without extensive personal data collection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beampipe provides standard website analytics reporting, including visits, page views, referral sources, and audience information, presented through a simplified dashboard rather than the dense interface typical of Google Analytics.

Beyond page views, it supports custom event tracking so site owners can measure actions like sign-ups, purchases, or button clicks, along with goal tracking to monitor conversion-oriented metrics over time.

A Slack integration lets teams receive analytics notifications and periodic (for example, daily) traffic reports directly in a Slack channel rather than requiring someone to log into a separate dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Beampipe's free plan covers up to 10,000 monthly page views across up to 5 domains, with no credit card required to sign up, making it accessible for personal sites, blogs, or early-stage projects.

The entry-level paid plan is priced at 10 dollars per month and increases the limits to 100,000 monthly page views across 20 domains, while also unlocking the Slack integration for notifications and reports.

Detailed information on any higher-volume paid tiers above the entry-level plan could not be fully confirmed during research, so prospective users with high-traffic sites should check current pricing directly on beampipe.io.

features.title: Cookie-less tracking
features.description: Tracks website traffic without relying on cookies, reducing the need for cookie-consent banners.

features.title: Privacy-regulation-oriented design
features.description: Built with GDPR, PECR, and CCPA compliance in mind for privacy-conscious site owners.

features.title: Custom event tracking
features.description: Track specific user actions such as sign-ups or purchases beyond basic page views.

features.title: Goal tracking
features.description: Define and monitor conversion-oriented goals within the analytics dashboard.

features.title: Slack integration
features.description: Send analytics notifications and periodic traffic reports directly into a Slack channel.

features.title: Multi-domain support
features.description: Track multiple websites under a single account, with domain limits varying by plan.

features.title: Simple, lightweight dashboard
features.description: A pared-down analytics interface focused on core traffic metrics rather than dense enterprise reporting.

pros: Free tier available for small sites with no credit card required
Cookie-less approach avoids the need for a cookie-consent banner in many cases
Simple, uncluttered dashboard compared to Google Analytics
Affordable entry-level paid plan at $10/month for moderate traffic
Slack integration is convenient for small teams who live in Slack
cons: Smaller company/team behind the product than major analytics providers, with limited public information on its scale or stability
Feature depth (segmentation, advanced funnels, etc.) is likely more limited than Google Analytics or larger paid competitors
Higher-volume pricing tiers were not fully verifiable during research
Less brand recognition and community size compared to competitors like Plausible or Fathom
Public information about the company/founder behind Beampipe is limited, which may concern some enterprise buyers

faqs.question: Is Beampipe still active in 2026?
faqs.answer: As of research, the beampipe.io website, documentation, blog, and associated social account were all live and displaying current pricing and content, indicating the service appears to be operating, though independent confirmation of recent business activity was limited.

faqs.question: Does Beampipe use cookies?
faqs.answer: No. Beampipe is a cookie-less analytics tool, designed to track site traffic without setting tracking cookies in visitors' browsers.

faqs.question: Is Beampipe free?
faqs.answer: Yes, for low-traffic sites. Beampipe offers a free plan for up to 10,000 monthly page views across up to 5 domains, with paid plans starting at $10/month for higher volume.

faqs.question: How does Beampipe compare to Google Analytics?
faqs.answer: Beampipe is positioned as a simpler, privacy-focused, cookie-less alternative to Google Analytics, with a lighter dashboard and less extensive feature depth.

faqs.question: Does Beampipe support custom events?
faqs.answer: Yes, Beampipe supports custom event tracking for actions like sign-ups or purchases, alongside goal tracking.

faqs.question: Does Beampipe integrate with Slack?
faqs.answer: Yes, Beampipe offers a Slack integration for analytics notifications and periodic traffic reports, available on its paid plans.

faqs.question: Is Beampipe GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: Beampipe states its cookie-less tracking approach is designed to be compliant with GDPR, the UK's PECR, and the US's CCPA.

faqs.question: Who owns or built Beampipe?
faqs.answer: Public information about the specific founder or team behind Beampipe is limited; available evidence suggests it was built and launched by a small, independent team around 2020.

enrichment.short_description: Beanstalk is a hosted Git and Subversion platform from Wildbit that combines source code hosting, code review, and automated deployment to servers and cloud platforms for small and mid-sized development teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (1 repository, 1 user); paid plans start at $15/month (Bronze)
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held company (Wildbit; exact employee count not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Beanstalk Review 2026: Git/SVN Hosting and Deployment Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Beanstalk is Wildbit's Git and SVN hosting platform with code review and automated deployment. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Beanstalk, at beanstalkapp.com, is a hosted Git and Subversion (SVN) platform that combines source code hosting, code review, and automated deployment in one product. It is made by Wildbit, LLC, a privately held, bootstrapped software company founded in 1999 and headquartered in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; Wildbit describes itself as profitable, debt-free, and privately owned, and it is also known for other products such as the transactional email service Postmark. Beanstalk itself launched in 2007, making it one of the older continuously operating players in the hosted version-control and deployment space, predating many later entrants that focused purely on Git after GitHub's own 2008 launch; Beanstalk notably supports both Git and Subversion, which some newer competitors do not. According to Beanstalk's own marketing materials, over 100,000 companies, ranging from small teams to larger organizations, have used the service since its 2007 launch. Core functionality includes in-browser code browsing and editing, branch management, repository and branch-level permissions, code review with issue tracking and discussion threads and coverage statistics, and deployment automation supporting multiple environments and targets including FTP, SFTP, shell-based servers, Cloud Files, Heroku, Amazon S3, and DreamObjects, along with release notes and deployment notifications; older integrations reference webhook support for chat tools like HipChat and Campfire, which reflects the product's long operating history and may indicate some older integrations are legacy rather than currently emphasized. Security features include stated bank-level encryption, two-step (two-factor) authentication, and SOC 1 Type 2 compliance. Pricing follows a subscription model with a free account offering 100MB of storage, 1 repository, and 1 user (no credit card required), and paid tiers beginning with Bronze at $15/month (3GB storage, 10 repositories, 5 users, 3 deployment servers per repository) and Silver at $25/month (6GB, 25 repositories, 20 users, 5 servers per repository), followed by higher enterprise-oriented tiers Gold ($50/month), Platinum ($100/month), and Diamond ($200/month), which add security tooling, IP restrictions, simultaneous multi-server deployments, and a stated 100 percent uptime SLA; annual billing offers a discount, and custom storage is available above the Diamond tier by request. As of the research date, beanstalkapp.com, its pricing page, and its status page were all live and displaying current-looking pricing and functionality, supporting that the service continues to operate, though this is based on the website being reachable rather than independently confirmed recent usage or company financial disclosures.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Beanstalk
paragraphs: Beanstalk is a hosted platform for Git and Subversion source code hosting, code review, and automated deployment, built by Wildbit, LLC, a privately held company founded in 1999 and headquartered in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

Beanstalk itself launched in 2007, positioning it as one of the longer-running services in the hosted version-control space, and notably one of the few that continues to support both Git and Subversion rather than Git alone.

According to Beanstalk's own materials, over 100,000 companies have used the platform since launch, spanning small development teams to larger organizations that need combined code hosting and deployment automation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Beanstalk provides in-browser code browsing and editing, branch creation and management, and repository- or branch-level permissions, alongside a code review workflow with issue tracking, threaded discussions, and coverage statistics.

Its deployment automation supports multiple environments and a range of targets, including FTP, SFTP, shell-based servers, Cloud Files, Heroku, Amazon S3, and DreamObjects, with release notes and deployment notifications built in.

On the security side, Beanstalk states it uses bank-level encryption, offers two-step authentication, and maintains SOC 1 Type 2 compliance, which is relevant for teams with compliance requirements around their source code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Beanstalk offers a free account (100MB storage, 1 repository, 1 user, no credit card required) alongside a tiered subscription structure starting with Bronze at $15/month and Silver at $25/month for smaller teams.

Larger or higher-security needs are served by Gold ($50/month), Platinum ($100/month), and Diamond ($200/month) tiers, which add features like IP restrictions, simultaneous multi-server deployments, and a stated 100 percent uptime SLA.
Annual billing is available at a discount over monthly billing, and Beanstalk offers custom storage arrangements for needs beyond the top-tier Diamond plan upon request.

features.title: Deployment notifications and release notes
features.description: Track deployment history with notifications and release notes for each deploy.

features.title: Two-step authentication
features.description: Adds an extra login verification step to help protect repository access.

features.title: SOC 1 Type 2 compliance
features.description: Meets SOC 1 Type 2 controls, relevant for teams with compliance or audit requirements.

pros: One of the few hosted platforms still supporting both Git and Subversion
Long operating history (since 2007) under a stable, profitable, bootstrapped company
Deployment automation covers a wide range of hosting targets beyond just Git pushes
Free tier available for solo developers or evaluation
Security posture includes two-step authentication and SOC 1 Type 2 compliance
cons: Pricing is per-repository/user-tiered and can get expensive for larger teams compared to flat-rate competitors
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket
Some referenced integrations (like HipChat and Campfire webhooks) reflect the product's age and may be legacy rather than current priorities
Free tier is quite limited (100MB storage, 1 repository, 1 user)
Less name recognition among developers today than more modern Git-hosting platforms

faqs.question: Is Beanstalk still operating in 2026?
faqs.answer: Based on the live beanstalkapp.com website, pricing page, and status page all showing current content as of research, Beanstalk appears to still be operating, though this is based on site availability rather than independently confirmed company financial data.

faqs.question: Who makes Beanstalk?
faqs.answer: Beanstalk is made by Wildbit, LLC, a privately held, bootstrapped company founded in 1999 and headquartered in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, which also makes the transactional email service Postmark.

faqs.question: Does Beanstalk support Subversion as well as Git?
faqs.answer: Yes. Beanstalk supports both Git and Subversion (SVN) repository hosting, which distinguishes it from many newer, Git-only hosting platforms.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Beanstalk?
faqs.answer: Yes, Beanstalk offers a free account with 100MB of storage, 1 repository, and 1 user, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does Beanstalk cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $15/month (Bronze tier) and scale up through Silver ($25/month), Gold ($50/month), Platinum ($100/month), and Diamond ($200/month), based on storage, repository, and user limits.

faqs.question: Does Beanstalk support automated deployment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Beanstalk can automatically deploy code to targets including FTP, SFTP, shell servers, Heroku, Amazon S3, Cloud Files, and DreamObjects.

faqs.question: When did Beanstalk launch?
faqs.answer: Beanstalk launched in 2007, according to the company's own marketing materials.

faqs.question: Is Beanstalk suitable for enterprise compliance needs?
faqs.answer: Beanstalk states it maintains SOC 1 Type 2 compliance and offers security features like two-step authentication and IP restrictions on higher tiers, which may suit teams with basic compliance requirements.

enrichment.short_description: Bearer is a developer-first static application security testing (SAST) tool for finding sensitive data flows and security risks in source code; it was acquired by Cycode in 2024 and its scanning engine now powers Cycode's ASPM platform, though the open-source Bearer CLI remains available.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open-source CLI); Cloud/enterprise pricing via Cycode is custom
enrichment.headquarters: Originally Clichy, France (Paris region); Bearer's team and technology are now part of Cycode
enrichment.seo_title: Bearer Review 2026: SAST Tool, Cycode Acquisition, Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bearer is a developer-first SAST tool for sensitive data and security risks in code, now part of Cycode. See 2026 status, features, and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bearer is a developer-first static application security testing (SAST) tool, originally built by a company also called Bearer, founded in 2018 by Guillaume Montard (CEO) and Cedric Fabianski, and headquartered near Paris, in Clichy, France. Bearer raised seed funding from investors including Alven, Kima Ventures, Partech, and Point Nine, with a reported total of 8 million dollars in equity financing across its seed rounds, including a second tranche announced in October 2022. The core product is the Bearer CLI, an open-source scanner that analyzes source code to discover, filter, and prioritize security and privacy risks, with particular emphasis on identifying flows of sensitive data such as personally identifiable information (PII) and protected health information (PHI) through an application's codebase, aimed at helping developers catch risky data handling before it reaches production. In March 2024, application security company Cycode announced it was acquiring Bearer, with the deal closing on April 30, 2024, explicitly framed as accelerating Cycode's move into AI-enhanced security remediation. As a result of the acquisition, Bearer's scanning engine, privacy-analysis capabilities, and engineering team moved into Cycode, and Bearer's technology now powers capabilities within Cycode's Application Security Posture Management (ASPM) platform. Bearer is no longer an independently operating company; its original bearer.sh website now redirects to bearer.com, which presents Bearer as a Cycode product rather than a standalone company. The Bearer CLI itself remains available and free, released under the Elastic License v2 (ELv2), and continues to receive active updates, evidenced by version releases into 2026, supporting languages including Ruby, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, and PHP, and integrating with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket for CI/CD pipeline scanning. Beyond the free CLI, more advanced capabilities, described as 'Bearer Pro' and offered through the broader Cycode ASPM platform, add cross-file and interprocedural analysis and support for additional languages such as C#, Kotlin, and Elixir; this paid tier is sold as part of Cycode's enterprise offering rather than as a standalone Bearer subscription, and Cycode does not publish self-service list pricing for it, consistent with typical enterprise security sales motions. In short, for anyone evaluating 'Bearer' in 2026, the correct framing is: independently-operating startup status ended in 2024 via acquisition, but the product itself (both the free open-source CLI and a paid enterprise tier) continues to exist and be actively developed under Cycode's ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bearer
paragraphs: Bearer is a developer-first static application security testing (SAST) tool designed to discover, filter, and prioritize security and privacy risks in source code, with a particular focus on tracing how sensitive data such as personal or health information flows through an application.

The Bearer product and company were founded in 2018 by Guillaume Montard and Cedric Fabianski, headquartered near Paris in Clichy, France, and the company raised approximately 8 million dollars in seed funding from investors including Alven, Kima Ventures, Partech, and Point Nine.

In 2024, application security company Cycode acquired Bearer, announcing the deal in March 2024 and closing it on April 30, 2024. Bearer no longer operates as an independent company; its scanning technology and team are now part of Cycode's Application Security Posture Management (ASPM) platform, and bearer.sh now redirects to bearer.com under Cycode's branding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Bearer's engine scans source code to identify data types being handled (including PII and PHI), maps how that data flows through the application, and flags risky patterns such as data being logged, sent to third parties, or stored insecurely.

The Bearer CLI supports scanning for languages including Ruby, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, and PHP, and integrates into developer workflows via GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, making it usable directly in CI/CD pipelines to catch issues before code reaches production.

Under Cycode, an expanded 'Bearer Pro' capability adds cross-file and interprocedural analysis (tracing data flows across multiple files and function calls) along with support for additional languages including C#, Kotlin, and Elixir, as part of Cycode's broader ASPM platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Bearer CLI remains free and open source, released under the Elastic License v2 (ELv2), and can be downloaded and run directly from its GitHub repository without a paid plan.

More advanced capabilities, sold as part of Cycode's ASPM platform rather than as a standalone Bearer product, are not available with published self-service pricing; interested organizations need to contact Cycode directly for a quote or demo.
Because Bearer is no longer sold as an independent product, there is no separate 'Bearer subscription' available outside of the Cycode platform as of the most recent available information.

features.title: Sensitive data flow mapping
features.description: Traces how personally identifiable and health information moves through an application's codebase.

features.title: Static application security testing (SAST)
features.description: Scans source code directly to find security and privacy risks without running the application.

features.title: Open-source CLI
features.description: A free command-line scanner released under the Elastic License v2, usable outside of any paid platform.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Scans Ruby, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, and PHP in the free CLI, with more languages available in the Pro tier.

features.title: CI/CD integration
features.description: Integrates with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket to run scans automatically in development pipelines.

features.title: Risk prioritization and filtering
features.description: Filters and ranks discovered risks to reduce noise and false positives for development teams.

features.title: Cross-file and interprocedural analysis (Pro)
features.description: Advanced analysis tracing data flows across multiple files and function calls, available through Cycode.

features.title: Part of Cycode's ASPM platform
features.description: Bearer's engine now contributes to Cycode's broader Application Security Posture Management product.

pros: Free, open-source CLI available with no cost barrier to start scanning code
Strong focus on sensitive data flow tracing (PII/PHI), which many general SAST tools do not emphasize as heavily
Backed by Cycode's resources post-acquisition, which may support more sustained development than an early-stage standalone startup
Integrates directly into common CI/CD platforms (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket)
Enterprise customer base (per Bearer's own site, names like Shopify, Datadog, and Bloomberg are cited) suggests real-world enterprise validation
cons: No longer an independently operating company, which may concern buyers who prefer standalone vendors
Advanced 'Pro' capabilities require adopting the broader Cycode ASPM platform rather than a lightweight standalone product
No public self-service pricing for anything beyond the free CLI
Free CLI language support is more limited than the paid tier's expanded language list
Brand transition from Bearer to Cycode may create confusion for users familiar with the original standalone product

faqs.question: Is Bearer still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No. Bearer was acquired by Cycode, with the deal announced in March 2024 and closed on April 30, 2024; its technology and team are now part of Cycode.

faqs.question: Is the Bearer CLI still free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Bearer CLI remains free and open source under the Elastic License v2 (ELv2) and is available on GitHub.

faqs.question: What does bearer.sh redirect to now?
faqs.answer: The original bearer.sh website redirects to bearer.com, which presents Bearer as a product within Cycode rather than a standalone company.

faqs.question: Who founded Bearer?
faqs.answer: Bearer was founded in 2018 by Guillaume Montard (CEO) and Cedric Fabianski, and was headquartered in Clichy, near Paris, France.

faqs.question: What does Bearer actually scan for?
faqs.answer: Bearer performs static application security testing (SAST) with a particular focus on mapping how sensitive data, such as PII and PHI, flows through an application's source code, alongside general security risk detection.

faqs.question: What languages does Bearer support?
faqs.answer: The free CLI supports Ruby, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, and PHP; the paid Bearer Pro capability through Cycode adds support for additional languages including C#, Kotlin, and Elixir.

faqs.question: How much does Bearer Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Cycode does not publish self-service list pricing for Bearer Pro or the broader ASPM platform; organizations need to contact Cycode directly for pricing.

faqs.question: Why did Cycode acquire Bearer?
faqs.answer: Cycode described the acquisition as accelerating its move into AI-enhanced security remediation, folding Bearer's scanning engine and privacy-analysis capabilities into its Application Security Posture Management (ASPM) platform.

use_cases.title: Sensitive data risk detection in code
use_cases.description: Security and engineering teams use the Bearer CLI to find where PII or PHI is handled insecurely in their codebase before it reaches production.

use_cases.title: CI/CD pipeline security scanning
use_cases.description: Teams integrate Bearer's scanning into GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket pipelines to automatically catch security and privacy risks on every code change.

use_cases.title: Enterprise ASPM adoption via Cycode
use_cases.description: Larger organizations adopt Bearer's capabilities as part of Cycode's broader ASPM platform for centralized application security posture management.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise security teams evaluating ASPM platforms

features: Ruby, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, PHP support
Part of Cycode's full ASPM platform
